meta.title: F12 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 6 Series F12 engine — N55, N57, N63, S63. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for the grand tourer convertible. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series F12 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F12 engine typically costs £4,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the N55 640i sits at £4,000–£7,000, while the S63 M6 V8 can reach £15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on engine and specialist location.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series F12 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 3.0-litre straight-six petrol is considered the most reliable engine in the F12 range — it's a proven, well-understood design with fewer major failure modes than the V8s. The N57 diesel is also highly reliable, though the EGR cooler is a known weak point [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series F12?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve stem seal wear on the N63 V8 is the most significant engine problem — blue smoke on startup is the classic warning sign. The S63 V8 in the M6 can suffer from rod bearing wear. The N57 diesel can have EGR cooler and DPF issues. The N55 is the most reliable engine in the range, though HPFP and VANOS issues can occur.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 6 Series F12 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N55 is the most reliable option — it's a proven engine with a strong track record. The N57 is also reliable, though emission system maintenance is important. The N63 V8 and S63 V8 are spectacular engines but require careful maintenance — valve stem seals and rod bearings are known weaknesses.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series F12 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is a hard avoid, but the N63 V8 requires very careful consideration — valve stem seal wear is well-documented and a full repair costs £1,500–£3,500 [BMW-QUOTE]. The S63 V8 also requires rod bearing replacement as preventative maintenance (£2,000–£3,500). For most buyers, the 640i or 640d is the sweet spot.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F12 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: On N63 V8s, blue smoke on startup indicates valve stem seal failure — investigate immediately. On S63 M6s, a low oil pressure warning or knocking sound is critical — stop driving immediately. On N55 640is, long cranking or limp mode indicates HPFP failure. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series F12 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 F12, a £4,500–£7,500 N57 recon is 30–50% — safe. Against a tired £8,000 example, the same recon is 56–94% — borderline. Our 70% BMW scrap threshold is the line [BMW-VERIFIED]. For M6 cars, even the most expensive replacement is viable given collector values.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 6 Series F12 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55 and N57 can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing — they are proven, durable designs. The N63 and S63 V8s can also achieve high mileages, but require more regular specialist attention — valve stem seals and rod bearings are critical maintenance items. All F12 engines are now 7+ years old.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F12, F13 and F06 6 Series?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F12 is the 2-door convertible (2011–2018), the F13 is the 2-door coupé (2011–2017), and the F06 is the 4-door Gran Coupé (2012–2018). All three share the L6 platform and most engines, but body styles differ. The G14 8 Series Convertible succeeded the F12 in 2018.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series F12 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block. Tell us your variant and production year, and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free. Note that the 650i power output increased from 407 PS to 450 PS with the LCI in 2014.

hero.h1: BMW 6 Series F12 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F12 Convertible — from the 3.0-litre N55 straight-six to the 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 and the S63-powered M6. Real UK costs and honest reliability data for BMW's luxurious grand tourer convertible. Backed by 120+ real UK F12 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 6 Series F12 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F12 Convertible specifically — spanning the N55 straight-six petrol, the N57 straight-six diesel, the N63 V8 petrol, the S63 V8 in the M6, and their LCI updates.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages. The N55 straight-six is a proven, reliable engine — it's been in production since 2009 and powers numerous BMW models. The N57 diesel six is also well-regarded for its refinement and durability. The N63 V8 has a reputation for higher running costs — valve stem seal wear is a known issue. The S63 V8 in the M6 is generally reliable, though rod bearing wear and turbocharger issues have been reported on early examples.

overview.intro: The F12 is the convertible body of BMW's third-generation 6 Series, unveiled at the 2011 Shanghai Auto Show. It shares the L6 platform with the F10 5 Series and F01 7 Series, offering a luxurious grand tourer experience with a fabric roof that operates at speeds up to 40 km/h. The engine line-up featured a 3.0-litre straight-six petrol (640i), a 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (640d), a 4.4-litre V8 petrol (650i), and the S63 V8-powered M6. A late 2014 LCI facelift brought an output boost for the 650i to 450 PS and subtle styling updates. The F12 was produced until September 2018, succeeded by the G14 8 Series Convertible.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L6 (shared with F10, F01) • Predecessor: E64 6 Series Convertible • Successor: G14 8 Series Convertible • Sibling body styles in this generation: F13 Coupé, F06 Gran Coupé.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The F12 Convertible generated 120+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], concentrated on the 640i petrol and 640d diesel as the range reaches affordable used-car territory.

items.quote: BMW's N55 single-turbo straight-six is a proven, reliable engine — smooth, responsive, and with a strong torque curve from 1,300 rpm. The 640i offers effortless grand touring performance without the V8's running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder refinement with sensible running costs.

items.quote: The N57 straight-six diesel is a masterpiece of refinement and efficiency — 313 PS, 630 Nm of torque, and genuine 40+ mpg capability. The 640d offers the best blend of performance and economy in the F12 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless torque and long-distance cruising capability.

items.quote: The N63 'hot-vee' V8 is a spectacular engine but has a reputation for heat-related failures — valve stem seal wear is the most common issue, leading to blue smoke on startup. The LCI's N63B44B is improved but still requires careful maintenance.
items.whoItsFor: 650i buyers with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The S63 V8 in the M6 is the most powerful and most expensive engine in the F12 range — rod bearing wear is a known issue on early examples, and a full replacement can exceed £15,000. The M6 is a future classic, but it comes with M-car running costs.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who want the ultimate F12 experience.

items.quote: The 640d offers near-650i performance with significantly lower running costs — 313 PS, 630 Nm of torque, and excellent fuel economy. It's the smart buy for anyone covering serious mileage in their grand tourer.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless performance with sensible economy.

items.quote: The 640i is often overlooked in favour of the V8 650i or the diesel 640d — but it's the sweet spot for many buyers. The N55 is proven and reliable, the power is more than adequate, and running costs are significantly lower than the V8. It's the most balanced engine in the F12 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder refinement without diesel clatter or V8 bills.

rows.ownershipVerdict: High Cost — spectacular V8, costly to maintain.

economicsRule.title: The F12 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500–£7,500 N57 recon against a £8,000–£25,000 F12 sits at 18–94% of the car's value【EM-VERIFIED】. On a sub-£10,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £15,000+ example, the same recon drops to 30–50% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For M6 cars (£20,000–£40,000+), even the most expensive S63 replacement is viable given collector values.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F12 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: N57 640d — excellent fuel economy, fewer major failure modes than the V8s.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F12 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: S63 M6 — rod bearings, V8 complexity and M-car running costs add up fast.

keyTakeaways.question: Which F12 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: N55 640i — proven six-cylinder reliability, strong performance, and lower running costs than the V8.

problems.title: N63 V8 Valve Stem Seal Wear
problems.rootCause: The N63 'hot-vee' design places the turbochargers inside the V of the engine, leading to heat-related valve stem seal hardening. This causes oil to leak past the seals into the combustion chamber, resulting in blue smoke on startup and increased oil consumption. BMW issued technical service bulletins for valve seal replacement.

tieredCosts.work: Valve stem seal replacement (specialist tool required)

problems.recommendation: If you're buying an N63-powered F12 650i, budget for valve stem seal replacement immediately unless there's documented proof it's already been done — £1,500–£3,500 now is far cheaper than the £6,000+ engine replacement that follows if ignored. Blue smoke on startup is the classic warning sign — don't ignore it.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your F12 650i →

problems.title: S63 Rod Bearing Wear (M6)
problems.rootCause: Early S63 engines can suffer from rod bearing wear — a known issue shared with the S65 V8 in the E9x M3. Oil starvation and clearance issues can lead to bearing failure and catastrophic engine damage. This is the single most important maintenance item on any S63-powered BMW.

tieredCosts.work: Rod bearing replacement with upgraded bearings

problems.recommendation: If you're buying an F12 M6, budget for rod bearing replacement immediately unless there's documented proof it's already been done — £2,000–£3,500 now is dramatically cheaper than the £9,000+ engine replacement that follows a bearing failure. This is not optional; it's the defining maintenance item of the S63 V8.

cta.label: Compare S63 engine prices for your F12 M6 →

problems.title: N55 HPFP & VANOS Issues
problems.rootCause: The N55 can suffer from high-pressure fuel pump (HPFP) failure and VANOS solenoid issues — though less common than on the earlier N54. HPFP failure leads to long cranking and limp mode; VANOS issues cause rough running and reduced performance.
problems.recommendation: Have the HPFP and VANOS system inspected at 60,000 miles — a £700–£1,500 preventative service is far cheaper than the £4,000+ replacement that follows a catastrophic failure. If you experience long cranking on startup, investigate immediately.

cta.label: Compare N55 engine prices for your F12 640i →

problems.title: N57 Diesel EGR Cooler & Emission System Issues
problems.rootCause: The N57 diesel's EGR cooler can develop internal leaks, allowing coolant to enter the exhaust system. DPF clogging (urban driving) and AdBlue system issues (where fitted) are also common. BMW has issued recalls for EGR cooler issues on some models.

tieredCosts.work: EGR cooler replacement, DPF clean, AdBlue system service
Full engine replacement if coolant contamination caused damage

problems.recommendation: Check your N57 F12's VIN against any outstanding EGR cooler recalls — if affected, the repair is free. For DPF health, ensure regular motorway driving to allow regeneration cycles to complete. Have the emission system inspected at 60,000 miles.

cta.label: Compare N57 diesel engine prices for your F12 640d →

replacementCosts.h2: F12 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the EM_Proprietary_Data baseline figures — F12 Convertible shares the L6 platform with the F10 5 Series, with similar engine-bay access, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

items.question: How much does a BMW 6 Series F12 engine replacement cost in the UK?
items.answer: A reconditioned F12 engine typically costs £4,000–£15,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on engine — the N55 640i sits at £4,000–£7,000, while the S63 M6 V8 can reach £15,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on engine and specialist location. Evaluate the car's value carefully before committing — for M6 cars, collector values may justify higher costs.

items.question: Which BMW 6 Series F12 engine is the most reliable?
items.answer: The N55 3.0-litre straight-six petrol (640i) is considered the most reliable engine in the F12 range — it's a proven, well-understood design with fewer major failure modes than the V8s. The N57 3.0-litre straight-six diesel (640d) is also highly reliable, though the EGR cooler is a known weak point [PROVISIONAL — pending dedicated Engine Page].

items.question: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series F12?
items.answer: Valve stem seal wear on the N63 V8 (650i) is the most significant engine problem — blue smoke on startup is the classic warning sign. The S63 V8 in the M6 can suffer from rod bearing wear. The N57 diesel can have EGR cooler and DPF issues. The N55 640i is the most reliable engine in the range, though HPFP and VANOS issues can occur.

items.question: Is the BMW 6 Series F12 reliable?
items.answer: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N55 640i is the most reliable option — it's a proven engine with a strong track record. The N57 640d is also reliable, though emission system maintenance is important. The N63 V8 and S63 V8 are spectacular engines but require careful maintenance — valve stem seals and rod bearings are known weaknesses. Buy a well-maintained example and budget for specialist servicing.

items.question: Which BMW 6 Series F12 engine should I avoid?
items.answer: No engine is a hard avoid, but the N63 V8 (650i) requires very careful consideration — valve stem seal wear is well-documented and a full repair costs £1,500–£3,500 [BMW-QUOTE]. The S63 V8 (M6) also requires rod bearing replacement as preventative maintenance (£2,000–£3,500). Without documented work, either V8 is a higher-risk purchase. For most buyers, the 640i or 640d is the sweet spot.

items.question: How do I know if my F12 engine needs replacing?
items.answer: On N63 V8s, blue smoke on startup indicates valve stem seal failure — investigate immediately. On S63 M6s, a low oil pressure warning or knocking sound is critical — stop driving immediately. On N55 640is, long cranking or limp mode indicates HPFP failure. On N57 640ds, reduced performance or warning lights may indicate EGR cooler or DPF issues. Use our diagnostic tool for a personalised assessment.

items.question: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series F12 engine, or should I scrap the car?
items.answer: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £15,000 F12, a £4,500–£7,500 N57 recon is 30–50% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £8,000 example, the same recon is 56–94% — borderline. Our 70% BMW scrap threshold is the line [BMW-VERIFIED]. For M6 cars (£20,000–£40,000+), even the most expensive S63 replacement is viable given collector values.

items.question: How long do BMW 6 Series F12 engines last?
items.answer: The N55 and N57 can exceed 150,000 miles with proper servicing — they are proven, durable designs. The N63 and S63 V8s can also achieve high mileages, but require more regular specialist attention — valve stem seals (N63) and rod bearings (S63) are critical maintenance items. All F12 engines are now 7+ years old — condition and history matter more than mileage.

items.question: What is the difference between the F12, F13 and F06 6 Series?
items.answer: The F12 is the 2-door convertible (2011–2018), the F13 is the 2-door coupé (2011–2017), and the F06 is the 4-door Gran Coupé (2012–2018). All three share the L6 platform and most engines, but body styles and some specifications differ. The F12's fabric roof operates at speeds up to 40 km/h. The G14 8 Series Convertible succeeded the F12 in 2018.

items.answer: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the engine block. Tell us your variant (640i, 640i xDrive, 650i, 650i xDrive, 640d, 640d xDrive, M6 Convertible), production year, and we'll identify it — 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.
